title: Whittled lime relief panel
subtitle: A simple low-relief botanical carving in seasoned lime
excerpt: A low-relief carving in seasoned lime showing a single botanical motif: a sprig, a leaf, or a simple flower. A first relief-carving project that teaches stop cuts, clearing cuts, and how to read a shallow relief surface.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for relief-carving technique and gouge selection. Early-twentieth-century art-school carving manuals from the school-tradition (Newey & Drage). The Mechanic's Workshop Companion series for surface decoration methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried or kiln-dried wood at a stable moisture content (8 to 12 percent for indoor use). Dimensionally stable.
The curvature of a gouge cutting edge, numbered from 1 (flat) to 11 (deep U). A No. 5 is a medium curve; a No. 3 is almost flat.
The rough-shaped piece of wood from which the finished item is carved.

content.text: Low-relief carving cuts into a flat
blank

to leave the motif standing proud of a recessed background. The motif here is a single botanical form: a sprig of three leaves, a stylised flower, or a curved stem with buds. Lime (basswood) is the carver's standard for relief work: the grain is fine and even, it holds fine detail well, and it cuts cleanly in any direction. Use a piece of
seasoned
lime 25 cm long, 15 cm wide, and 25 mm thick.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood

Lime (Tilia spp.) is the sculptor's standard for fine carving in northern Europe. The close, even grain cuts cleanly in any direction without tearing, which makes it forgiving for a first relief project. It is pale, almost white when fresh-cut, and takes a Danish oil finish well.
If lime is unavailable, sycamore is the next option. Avoid oak for a first relief project: the open grain makes fine edges difficult to achieve.
Drawing and outlining the motif
Draw the motif lightly in pencil. Keep it simple: three leaves on a curved stem, each leaf 5 cm long. The relief depth is 4 mm; the motif stands 4 mm above the recessed background.

Use the V-tool to outline the motif. Set the V-tool on the drawn line with the V centred on the line and make a push cut all the way around the outer edge of each leaf and the stem. These cuts set the boundary.
Lowering the background
Use the No. 5 gouge (medium
sweep

) to remove the background wood to a depth of 4 mm. Push cuts working away from the motif boundary. Start 5 mm back from the V-tool outline and work toward the edges of the panel first; then come back to clean up the area immediately around the motif.

Use the No. 3 gouge (nearly flat) to level the background. Push cuts across the grain of the background plane. The background does not need to be completely smooth; light gouge marks in a regular pattern are characteristic of traditional relief carving and look deliberate.
Shaping the motif surface

The raised leaves can be left flat or given a slight dome. For a domed leaf, use the No. 5 gouge along the leaf length with light push cuts to round the surface from the centreline outward. Keep the leaf edge thin at the perimeter.
Use the V-tool to cut the central vein of each leaf and to refine the stem where it intersects the background. A slicing cut angle of 20 degrees produces a crisp V-groove.
Finishing
Do not sand the carved surface. Sanding rounds the crisp edges that make relief carving legible. Apply two thin coats of Danish oil with a cloth, letting each coat dry before the next.
Variations
Geometric motif: a simple lattice or chevron pattern lowered to the same 4 mm depth. Useful for practising straight-line gouge control.
Deeper relief (10 mm): gives a more sculptural shadow effect. Requires a deeper-sweep gouge for roughing and more careful background levelling.

rows.symptom: Motif edges broke off when lowering the background
rows.cause: Gouge cut into the motif boundary rather than stopping before it
rows.fix: Always start background cuts 5 mm back from the motif edge. Take thin shavings near the boundary. Set a stop cut with the V-tool at the motif edge before taking any background cut near it.

rows.symptom: Background surface looks rough and uneven
rows.cause: Gouge cuts going against the grain in some areas
rows.fix: Lime can be cut in most directions, but there is still a preferred direction. Look for the cut that produces a clean shaving; reverse if the surface tears.

rows.symptom: V-tool catches and breaks out chips instead of cutting a clean line
rows.cause: Edge dull or too much force applied
rows.fix: Strop the V-tool on the leather strop, paying attention to both faces of the V. Use very light pressure; the V-tool cuts with the weight of your hand, not a push.
